title: Fooling intersections of low-weight halfspaces
speaker: Rocco Servedio
abstract: A weight-$t$ halfspace is a Boolean function $f(x)=\mathrm{sign}(w_1 x_1 + \cdots + w_n x_n - \theta)$ where each $w_i$ is an integer in $\{-t,\dots,t\}.$ We give an explicit pseudorandom generator that $\delta$-fools any intersection of $k$ weight-$t$ halfspaces with seed length poly$(\log n, \log k,t,1/\delta)$. In particular, our result gives an explicit PRG that fools any intersection of any quasipoly$(n)$ number of halfspaces of any polylog$(n)$ weight to any $1/$polylog$(n)$ accuracy using seed length polylog$(n).$

proof_stages.label: Boolean halfspaces, weights, and PRG-fooling setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the Boolean-cube setting, defines intersections of k integer-weight halfspaces with maximum coefficient weight t, and recalls the notion of an explicit PRG that fools a class by matching acceptance probabilities up to epsilon.

proof_stages.label: Main seed-length theorem and algorithmic meaning
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated as an explicit PRG for intersections of k weight-t halfspaces with seed length polynomial in log n, log k, t, and 1/epsilon, yielding new nontrivial cases and a deterministic approximate counting algorithm for bounded-coefficient 0-1 integer programs.

proof_stages.label: Prior bounds and the regularity/weight comparison
proof_stages.summary: Previous work is positioned as either fooling arbitrary functions of k halfspaces with linear dependence on k or fooling intersections of tau-regular halfspaces with polylogarithmic k-dependence, while low weight and regularity are shown to be incomparable as stated.

proof_stages.label: Central-limit/hybrid framework for halfspace PRGs
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews how Berry-Esseen and Lindeberg replacement proofs for regular linear forms lead to PRGs via Meka-Zuckerman hashing into buckets, and how a regularity lemma extends the single-halfspace construction beyond the regular case.

proof_stages.label: Extension to intersections and the k-regularity barrier
proof_stages.summary: Applying the CLT-to-PRG program to k-tuples explains the prior split: CLTs and derandomization can have good polylog k dependence for regular intersections, but regularity-lemma reductions for arbitrary intersections inherently seem to incur polynomial or linear dependence on k.

proof_stages.label: Low-weight regular-or-sparse reduction
proof_stages.summary: The new approach uses the elementary dichotomy that a weight-t halfspace is either epsilon-regular or sparse, decomposing a low-weight intersection into an intersection of regular halfspaces AND a small-width CNF and thereby changing the pseudorandomness target.

proof_stages.label: Coupled bucket hybrid for the regular-plus-CNF class
proof_stages.summary: To analyze the same bucketed generator for the regular-halfspace part combined with the CNF part, the proof couples the truly random and pseudorandom fillings of each bucket using CNF-fooling, then controls the resulting loss of independence and exact moment matching via mixture decompositions and stronger parameters.

proof_stages.label: Result recap and parameter-improvement goals
proof_stages.summary: The talk concludes by restating the low-weight intersection PRG, emphasizing the coupled CLT-style hybrid proof, and identifying future goals such as replacing the polynomial t and 1/epsilon dependence by logarithmic or no weight dependence and polylogarithmic accuracy dependence.

examples.name: 0-1 integer-program feasible set/counting
examples.note: The feasible solutions of a 0-1 integer program are presented as a polytope/intersection of halfspaces, and later as an algorithmic interpretation of enumerating the PRG seeds to estimate the number of feasible Boolean points.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the PRG result to 0-1 integer-program counting, explaining enumeration over seeds, the matrix constraints, running time, and the resulting deterministic estimate.

passes.reason: A 0-1 IP feasible set is an ordinary Boolean polytope/application of halfspace intersections, used illustratively rather than adversarially.

examples.name: Hamming-distance avoidance from forbidden Boolean points
examples.note: A Boolean function accepting exactly those cube points at some minimum Hamming distance from each forbidden point illustrates that weight-one halfspace intersections express more than standard CNFs.
examples.engagement_reason: the forbidden Hamming-distance Boolean function is only cited as something expressible by weight-one halfspaces, with no construction or verification shown.

passes.reason: Avoiding arbitrary forbidden Boolean points by Hamming distance is a standard weight-one halfspace construction, not structurally sporadic or used to refute a claim.

examples.name: Majorities and intersections of majorities
examples.note: Majority gates and their intersections serve as a familiar low-weight subclass, and the majority function is later used to make the regular branch of the regular/sparse dichotomy concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: majorities and intersections of majorities are named as functions captured by low-weight halfspaces, but the speaker does not work through them.

passes.reason: Majority gates have equal unit weights and permutation symmetry, and are cited as positive examples captured by low-weight halfspaces.

examples.name: Dictator halfspace x1
examples.note: The halfspace depending only on x1 illustrates both that low weight need not imply regularity and that the Berry-Esseen Gaussian approximation fails without a regularity condition.
examples.engagement_reason: the dictator halfspace x1 is analyzed as a nonregular low-weight example, including its worst regularity and its step-function CDF showing failure of Gaussian closeness.

passes.reason: The dictator halfspace depends on only one coordinate and has worst-possible regularity, and is used to show regularity is necessary / low weight need not imply regularity.
the dictator x1 is a one-coordinate minimal halfspace with worst possible regularity, deployed to show low weight need not imply regularity

examples.name: n halfspaces with distinct heavy coordinates
examples.note: The cartoon family where the ith halfspace is nonregular because of coordinate xi exposes why making every halfspace regular may require fixing all n variables, motivating a different approach for polylogarithmic dependence on k.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through the example of n halfspaces with different heavy coordinates to show why making them all regular would require restricting all n variables.

passes.reason: The tuple with one distinct heavy coordinate per halfspace is a worst-case obstruction forcing restriction of all n variables in the regularity-lemma approach.
the n halfspaces with separate heavy coordinates form a worst-case barrier requiring restriction of all n variables, used against a hoped-for polylog-k regularity reduction
The tuple with distinct heavy coordinates attains the worst case of needing all n variables restricted, obstructing a polylog-k regularity reduction.
The tuple with a different heavy coordinate in each of n halfspaces is a worst-case diagonal obstruction requiring all variables to be restricted in the multi-regularity approach.
the construction maximizes distinct offending heavy coordinates and is used as an obstruction to a polylog-k regularity-lemma approach
The tuple spreads the dominant coordinate over all n variables, a worst-case obstruction forcing restriction of all variables in the regularity-lemma approach.
